← → or scroll
Dee Brown · 1970

Bury My Heart
at Wounded Knee

A Study in Greed, Betrayal & the Dakota Nation

Overarching Theme
"
Greed is a map with no destination.

The United States always wanted more — and maps, treaties,
and promises were just tools to take it.

03 Key Figure

Who was
Little Crow?

  • Born Taoyateduta, chief of the Mdewakanton Sioux
  • Signed treaties, adopted settler dress, and tried diplomacy
  • Believed resistance was futile, but not total submission
04 1851

The Treaties

  • Treaty of Traverse des Sioux: Dakota surrender ~24 million acres of Minnesota
  • Promised: small reservation strip, food, annuities
  • Told they could coexist peacefully
The U.S. never intended to reach the "destination" of peace — the map was always being redrawn.
05 1862

The Annuities
Don't Arrive

  • Payments are overdue — Dakota families are starving
  • Traders refuse further food credit
  • Hunting grounds decimated by the small reserve
Trader Andrew Myrick: "If they are hungry, let them eat grass."

Connection to theme: greed prevented collective prosperity.

06 The Breaking Point

Little Crow's
Dilemma

  • Younger Dakota warriors demand action
  • Pressured into "striking first"
  • Trapped: fight or risk starvation
A man caught between two worlds — respected by neither, abandoned by both.
07 August 1862

Dakota War
of 1862

  • Dakota warriors attack settlers across southwestern Minnesota
  • Over 400–800 settlers killed; Little Crow leads raids
  • U.S. government responds with military force
800+
Settlers killed
Aug
Month it began
1862
Year
08 September 1862

The Defeat

  • Dakotas defeated in September 1862
  • 303 sentenced to death — largest mass execution in U.S. history
  • Lincoln reduces the count to 38 hanged at Mankato
  • Little Crow shot dead while in Canada
09 Connecting the Dots

Back to the
Theme

  • The U.S. always wanted more land — and rewrote maps, literally and metaphorically
  • Greed had no destination — it only led to more greed
  • Maps, treaties, promises — all just weapons to dispossess
The Dakota weren't just starved and killed — they were erased from the map they once were.
Conclusion
"We are only little herds of buffaloes left scattered... the great herds that once covered the prairies are no more."

— Little Crow, War Speech, 1862

Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee
